text:
HEMATOLOGY/MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP VISIT
ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY ASSESSMENT -- AFTER CYCLE 4 OF 6
GEMCITABINE + CAPECITABINE (ESPAC-4 REGIMEN)


CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old West African woman with pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) of the head/uncinate process, now in the adjuvant phase of treatment following successful multimodality therapy including neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX, consolidative chemoradiation, and R0 Whipple resection. She presents today for her scheduled clinic visit and review after completing cycle 4 of planned 6 cycles of adjuvant gemcitabine plus capecitabine.


HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Osei-Bonsu was diagnosed with borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma (T4 N1 M0, stage III per AJCC 8th edition) several months ago. Her CA 19-9 at diagnosis was markedly elevated at 1,247 U/mL. She was treated with neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) for 8 cycles with progressive decline in CA 19-9 to 85 U/mL and meaningful radiographic shrinkage of the primary tumor. She subsequently underwent consolidative chemoradiation with conventionally fractionated IMRT (50.4 Gy in 28 fractions) and concurrent capecitabine, which was reasonably well tolerated. Following further disease response on restaging imaging, she underwent pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) approximately 4.5 months ago. Final surgical pathology was highly favorable: R0 resection on all margins, ypT2 ypN0 M0, College of American Pathologists (CAP) tumor regression score 2. Her postoperative course was complicated by a grade B pancreatic fistula that resolved with conservative management (TPN, octreotide, prolonged drain retention) and delayed discharge to postoperative day 18. She recovered well and by her 6-week postoperative visit her drain had been removed, pancreatic exocrine insufficiency was identified and addressed with pancrelipase (PERT), and new-onset post-Whipple insulin-dependent diabetes was being co-managed with endocrinology.

She initiated adjuvant gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 8, and 15 and capecitabine 1660 mg/m2/day orally in divided doses on days 1-21 of each 28-day cycle (ESPAC-4 regimen). After cycles 1-3, she developed grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia), prompting a 25% capecitabine dose reduction to 1250 mg/m2/day. At cycle 3 review she had mild, transient neutropenia without fever. Anastomotic fluid collection seen on early postoperative imaging had decreased on her cycle 3 surveillance scan and CA 19-9 was 32 U/mL at that time.

Today she presents after cycle 4. She completed cycle 4 on schedule without any dose delays. She reports her energy level is somewhat low -- "I get tired easily, but I can still manage around the house and take short walks" -- but is otherwise managing. She denies nausea or vomiting. Her appetite is fair; she has been using oral nutritional supplements with most meals. Her stool frequency has been roughly normal on the PERT regimen, with soft but formed stools. She denies significant abdominal pain, though she does report mild right upper quadrant discomfort intermittently that she attributes to postoperative changes; she does not characterize this as severe or progressive. Her hands and feet have improved since the capecitabine dose reduction -- mild redness and sensitivity remain on the palms but she denies blistering or pain that limits walking or daily activities. She also reports continued mild tingling in the fingertips and toes, which she attributes to residual neuropathy from her prior oxaliplatin exposure. She denies fever, chills, or signs of infection.

Her glycemic control has been a challenge. She reports more frequent hypoglycemic episodes in the past few weeks, which she and her endocrinologist have been adjusting her insulin regimen to address. She is checking her blood sugars at home and has logbook data with her today. She denies any emergency room visits for hypoglycemia.


REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
- Constitutional: Persistent grade 2 fatigue, no fevers, no night sweats, no chills. Weight loss from prior surgery has largely stabilized; no further significant weight loss since last visit.
- HEENT: No oral ulcers or mucositis currently. No blurred vision.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no dyspnea on exertion at her current activity level.
- Pulmonary: No cough, no shortness of breath.
- Gastrointestinal: Mild intermittent right upper quadrant discomfort, no severe abdominal pain. Stools soft, approximately 2x/day, no frank blood, no melena, no steatorrhea with current PERT dosing. No nausea or vomiting.
- Musculoskeletal/Neurologic: Bilateral distal hand and foot paresthesias, grade 1 (mild, not limiting activities), stable. No new focal neurologic complaints.
- Dermatologic: Mild palmar erythema and tenderness bilaterally, grade 1 hand-foot syndrome (improved from grade 2 prior to dose reduction). No significant plantar involvement at this time.
- Genitourinary: No dysuria, no hematuria. Urinary frequency unchanged.
- Hematologic: No unusual bruising or bleeding. No petechiae reported.
- Endocrine: Hypoglycemic episodes as noted above, being addressed with endocrinology.
- Psychiatric: Reports mild anxiety about disease recurrence, is coping reasonably well and has family support. Denies significant depression.


PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/78 mmHg. Heart rate 74 bpm, regular. Respiratory rate 16. Temperature 36.7°C. SpO2 97% on room air. Weight: 58.4 kg (down approximately 3.5 kg from her pre-surgical weight of approximately 62 kg; stable compared to last visit). Height 162 cm. BMI 22.3.

General: Well-appearing woman, appropriately groomed, in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3.

HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no oral lesions or mucositis. Sclerae anicteric. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical or supraclavicular chains.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.

Abdomomen: Well-healed midline surgical scar from the Whipple procedure with no wound complications. Abdomen soft, mildly tender to deep palpation in the right upper quadrant, non-peritoneal. No rebound, no guarding. No palpable hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. No ascites clinically (no shifting dullness, no fluid wave). No palpable masses.

Extremities: Mild bilateral palmar erythema with slight peeling of the skin at the fingertip creases -- consistent with grade 1 hand-foot syndrome. No blistering. No significant plantar involvement. No edema.

Neurological: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves grossly intact. Mild reduction in light touch sensation at the bilateral fingertips and distal toes on clinical testing, consistent with grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy (oxaliplatin-related). No ataxia. Deep tendon reflexes mildly diminished at bilateral ankles but present at knees.

Skin: No jaundice. No rash other than the palmar changes noted above.


LABORATORY RESULTS
Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 4.2 x10^9/L (normal range 4.0-11.0)
- ANC: 2.1 x10^9/L (adequate, no clinically significant neutropenia at present)
- Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL (grade 1 anemia; mildly decreased from 11.4 at cycle 3 -- likely multifactorial: chronic disease, cumulative chemotherapy effect, nutritional factors)
- Platelets: 148 x10^9/L (recovered from the nadir of 72 x10^9/L reported during cycle 3; currently above threshold for full gemcitabine dosing)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 3.9 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL; eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL (normal; no evidence of biliary obstruction at the hepaticojejunostomy)
- AST: 28 U/L, ALT: 31 U/L (both within normal limits)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 74 U/L (within normal range)
- Albumin: 3.3 g/dL (mildly decreased; nutritional support ongoing)

Tumor Markers:
- CA 19-9: 32 U/mL (continued downtrend from baseline 1,247 U/mL at diagnosis; was 85 U/mL at end of neoadjuvant therapy; 48 U/mL post-Whipple nadir; 32 U/mL at cycle 3 -- now reconfirmed at 32 U/mL, stable since last measurement; remains below the upper limit of normal of 37 U/mL)
- CEA: 2.3 ng/mL (normalized from 8.4 at diagnosis; within normal range)

Endocrine/Metabolic:
- HbA1c: 8.2% (suboptimal but improved from 7.8% pre-operatively, acknowledging this is now post-Whipple insulin-dependent diabetes with distinct pathophysiology)
- Fasting glucose: 142 mg/dL today (somewhat elevated; patient and endocrinology actively adjusting insulin regimen)

Coagulation: PT/INR within normal limits (patient not on anticoagulation).

Fecal elastase was checked at the 6-week postoperative visit and confirmed pancreatic exocrine insufficiency (<200 mcg/g). Current PERT dosing appears adequate based on symptom control (no steatorrhea); repeat testing not indicated today.


IMAGING RESULTS
Most recent surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis (obtained approximately one month ago, during cycle 3 of adjuvant chemotherapy): Post-Whipple anatomy with no evidence of disease recurrence. Anastomotic fluid collection noted on early postoperative imaging had decreased to 2.8 cm from 3.2 cm previously and was simple in appearance with no internal debris or gas. The surgical bed showed expected post-operative fibrosis without nodular enhancement or mass. No hepatic lesions, no peritoneal nodularity, no enlarged lymph nodes. CT chest at that time showed no pulmonary nodules.

No new imaging has been obtained since the cycle 3 scan. Per our surveillance protocol, next CT chest/abdomen/pelvis is planned at approximately the end of treatment (after cycle 6) or sooner if clinically indicated. Today's visit is a clinical and laboratory assessment without a scheduled scan.


ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman with PDAC of the pancreatic head/uncinate process (originally stage III, T4N1M0, borderline resectable) who has responded remarkably well to her multimodality treatment program. She has completed 4 of 6 planned cycles of adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine (ESPAC-4 regimen) following curative-intent R0 Whipple resection (ypT2 N0 M0, CAP regression score 2). CA 19-9 remains well within the normal range at 32 U/mL, and imaging continues to show no evidence of recurrence.

1. PANCREATIC DUCTAL ADENOCARCINOMA -- NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE
This is an excellent result. CA 19-9 has dropped from 1,247 U/mL at diagnosis to 32 U/mL today -- a reduction of greater than 97% -- and has been below the upper limit of normal since completing adjuvant therapy. The most recent imaging shows no suspicious findings in the surgical bed, liver, or chest. Clinically, she has no symptoms concerning for recurrence (no new abdominal pain, no jaundice, no weight loss beyond what was established post-operatively). I reviewed her overall disease trajectory with her today and emphasized that her pathologic response to neoadjuvant therapy (CAP score 2, ypN0, R0 resection) puts her in a favorable prognostic tier relative to average PDAC outcomes. We discussed, however, that PDAC carries meaningful recurrence risk and the importance of maintaining surveillance cannot be overstated. She understands.

2. ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY -- CYCLES 5 AND 6 PLANNING
She has completed 4 of 6 cycles of gemcitabine plus capecitabine. Her counts today are adequate for proceeding: ANC 2.1, platelets 148. She is currently on modified capecitabine dosing (1250 mg/m2/day, 75% of standard) due to prior grade 2 hand-foot syndrome, with good tolerability at this dose level (grade 1 HFS currently). Gemcitabine dosing has been full per plan so far.
- Plan: Proceed with cycle 5 starting today per protocol. Gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 IV days 1, 8, 15; capecitabine 1250 mg/m2/day (reduced dose, days 1-21). No further dose modification at this time.
- Continue to monitor CBC on days 8 and 15 of cycle 5 given thrombocytopenia previously seen during cycle 3 (platelet nadir 72). If platelets fall below 75 on day 8 or 15, hold gemcitabine and check CBC one week later prior to resuming.
- Discussed with patient the expected course: 2 more cycles remaining; she is eager to complete therapy and transition to surveillance.

3. HAND-FOOT SYNDROME (CAPECITABINE-RELATED)
Currently grade 1, well improved from the grade 2 episode that prompted dose reduction during cycles 1-3. On exam today: mild bilateral palmar erythema with superficial peeling, no blistering, no significant pain or functional impairment. No plantar involvement.
- Continue current capecitabine dose reduction at 1250 mg/m2/day.
- Counsel patient to continue urea-based or thick emollient cream application to hands and feet twice daily (she confirmed she is using this).
- Avoid tight footwear and repetitive friction. Continue to avoid hot water exposure.
- If grade 2 HFS recurs, hold capecitabine until resolution to grade 1 or less, then resume at same reduced dose.

4. PERIPHERAL SENSORY NEUROPATHY (OXALIPLATIN-RELATED)
Grade 1, bilateral distal extremities, stable and slowly improving compared to the peak grade 2 neuropathy during mFOLFIRINOX. Current gemcitabine/capecitabine regimen does not carry significant additional neuropathy risk. No dose adjustments needed for neuropathy at this time.
- Reassure patient that oxaliplatin-related neuropathy often continues to improve slowly over months to years following cessation of the drug.
- No pharmacologic intervention (duloxetine, gabapentin) indicated at grade 1 without functional impairment.

5. FATIGUE
Grade 2 by patient report, chronic, multifactorial (post-surgical recovery, chemotherapy, mild anemia, diabetes management challenges, post-Whipple physiologic changes). This is the symptom most affecting her quality of life.
- Encourage graduated physical activity -- even short daily walks -- which has evidence for reducing cancer-related fatigue.
- Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL; not severe enough to warrant erythropoiesis-stimulating agents or transfusion. Ensure adequate iron, B12, folate (she is post-GI surgery with altered anatomy).
- Ordered iron studies and B12/folate levels today to evaluate for nutritional contributors to anemia.
- Will reassess after cycle 6 to determine whether fatigue improves with treatment completion.

6. NUTRITIONAL STATUS AND PANCREATIC EXOCRINE INSUFFICIENCY
Albumin mildly low at 3.3 g/dL, weight stable but approximately 3.5 kg below pre-surgical baseline. She is using PERT (pancrelipase) with each meal and snack, and reports no steatorrhea. Oral nutritional supplementation is ongoing.
- Continue PERT at current dosing (pancrelipase 40,000 lipase units per meal; 20,000 units with snacks).
- Continue oral nutritional supplements (e.g., Ensure or equivalent) at least twice daily.
- Referral reminder: Reinforce follow-up with dietitian/nutritionist. A referral was placed previously; confirm patient has scheduled her appointment.
- Goal: return toward pre-treatment weight over the coming months with improved nutritional intake post-chemotherapy completion.

7. POST-WHIPPLE INSULIN-DEPENDENT DIABETES MELLITUS
HbA1c 8.2%; she is experiencing some hypoglycemic episodes, suggesting insulin dosing needs further refinement. Post-pancreatectomy diabetes (type 3c DM) is notoriously difficult to manage due to loss of both insulin and glucagon from residual pancreatic tissue.
- Continue endocrinology co-management. She has a follow-up appointment scheduled with endocrinology in 2 weeks.
- Reviewed the importance of consistent carbohydrate intake, especially during chemotherapy when appetite and intake can fluctuate, as a strategy to reduce hypoglycemia risk.
- Fasting glucose 142 today; BG log reviewed -- she has hypoglycemia approximately 2-3 times per week at nighttime, which she has been logging. Will defer specific insulin adjustments to endocrinology team.

8. ANASTOMOTIC FLUID COLLECTION
Was 2.8 cm (decreased from 3.2 cm) on cycle 3 scan, simple-appearing. No clinical symptoms to suggest infection or complication. This is expected to continue resolving passively.
- No intervention indicated.
- Will reassess on the planned end-of-treatment CT scan.

9. GERMLINE ATM VARIANT
Patient is a confirmed heterozygous carrier of a germline pathogenic ATM frameshift variant (p.V2716fs), identified on germline testing following the tumor molecular profiling. She was previously counseled on the implications for herself (modest increased risk of other ATM-associated malignancies, e.g., breast cancer surveillance consideration) and her first-degree relatives.
- Reiterate recommendation for genetic counseling for first-degree family members.
- Discussed breast cancer surveillance; will coordinate with primary care for baseline mammogram if not recently performed.

10. HYPERTENSION AND TYPE 2 DIABETES (PRE-EXISTING)
Blood pressure well-controlled today at 128/78 mmHg. Metformin was presumably held peri-operatively and may not be appropriate post-Whipple given insulin dependence; confirm with endocrinology.

11. SURVEILLANCE PLANNING
Following completion of cycle 6, plan for end-of-treatment CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast and CA 19-9. If no evidence of recurrence, transition to formal surveillance schedule:
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis + CA 19-9 + CEA every 3-4 months for first 2 years post-surgery.
- Then every 6 months for years 3-5.
- Oncology clinic follow-up every 3 months during active surveillance phase.
- Cancer survivorship program referral was placed at treatment completion visit; confirm patient has received intake appointment.

RETURN TO CLINIC
Patient will return in 4 weeks (approximately day 28 of cycle 5) for cycle 6 day 1 and clinical reassessment. She will have CBC checked on days 8 and 15 of this cycle at her local laboratory with results faxed to our clinic for review. She has my contact information and the triage nurse line in case of fever, severe nausea/vomiting, signs of bleeding, or significant worsening of symptoms. She verbalized understanding.

Time spent in clinic today: approximately 45 minutes (including examination and patient counseling).

Electronically signed by:
Attending Medical Oncologist, Gastrointestinal Oncology Program
